input.query_title: Turing patterns in network-organized activator-inhibitor systems
input.query_abstract: Turing instability in activator-inhibitor systems provides a paradigm of nonequilibrium pattern formation; it has been extensively investigated for biological and chemical processes. Turing pattern formation should furthermore be possible in network-organized systems, such as cellular networks in morphogenesis and ecological metapopulations with dispersal connections between habitats, but investigations have so far been restricted to regular lattices and small networks. Here we report the first systematic investigation of Turing patterns in large random networks, which reveals their striking difference from the known classical behavior. In such networks, Turing instability leads to spontaneous differentiation of the network nodes into activator-rich and activator-low groups, but ordered periodic structures never develop. Only a subset of nodes having close degrees (numbers of links) undergoes differentiation, with its characteristic degree obeying a simple general law. Strong nonlinear restructuring process leads to multiple coexisting states and hysteresis effects. The final stationary patterns can be well understood in the framework of the mean-field approximation for network dynamics.
input.hyp_generic_object: coupled phosphorus-recycling and hypolimnetic-oxygen dynamics across a network of hydrologically connected lakes
input.hyp_abstract: Chains and networks of lakes and reservoirs are hydrologically linked by channels and groundwater exchange, allowing dissolved constituents to move between basins. We ask whether an initially uniform, clear-water network can spontaneously split into eutrophic and clear basins without any external gradient in nutrient loading. Each basin is described by two coupled variables: a self-amplifying pool of recycled phosphorus sustained by sediment internal loading, and a suppressing hypolimnetic oxygen-and-mixing variable that damps that recycling; inter-basin exchange along the flow network is represented by the weighted graph Laplacian in place of a continuous dispersal term. A linear stability analysis about the uniform low-nutrient equilibrium, with disturbances decomposed onto Laplacian eigenmodes, produces a discrete dispersion relation. When the oxygenating variable is exchanged through the network appreciably faster than phosphorus, the homogeneous clear state, resilient to basin-wide perturbations, destabilizes along particular eigenmodes, and the network differentiates into persistently turbid and clear basins rather than a smooth trophic gradient. Only basins whose hydrologic connectivity lies near a critical value flip, following a simple threshold law we derive. The nonlinear internal-loading feedback yields alternative stable network configurations and marked hysteresis under gradually changing external inputs, implying that restoration targets are path dependent. A connectivity-class mean-field model predicts the fraction of basins that turn eutrophic.
input.candidate_abstract: The extreme eigenvalues of connectivity matrices govern the influence of the network structure on a number of network dynamical processes. A fundamental open question is whether the eigenvalues of large networks are well represented by ensemble averages. Here we investigate this question explicitly and validate the concept of ensemble averageability in random scale-free networks by showing that the ensemble distributions of extreme eigenvalues converge to peaked distributions as the system size increases. We discuss the significance of this result using synchronization and epidemic spreading as example processes.
